speaker: interviewer
text: Beginning tape number three, interview with Mr. Harry Schlesinger. It is March, the 30th, 1997. Mr. Schlesinger, before we get into the story of what you saw, with the Gypsies, I just want to sort of run through time-- in time order of what happened. You said you were-- that Dr. Mengele was calling out for twins.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that, at that time, you and your sister came forward.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Run it through. Run it through for me, in sequence order, where did you go from there, who did you see, and what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They was putting us twins in a separate place. And then came a car, a closed van. And it took all these twins. The girls were taken to the lady's hospital department, and the boys were taken to the men's. They call it MKL, Men's Konzentrations Lager. And the other one was the ladies'. And they-- they started the experiments. The main idea, for Mengele, to make these experiments, the German government wanted to find out how fast to double their army or maybe how fast to triple their army. So those days, they didn't know about four or five children, like today. But most of them were twins or triplets.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he figured-- they figured, the Germans, if a year is-- we have a million children born, if we have all twins, there will be a double amount of children. In Germany, that was the main idea to produce more children. 13 and 14-year-old girls, in Germany, they had an illegitimate, illegal child, they got a medal, because they brought, for the army, a child, for the German nation. So that's how he wanted to find out, how fast could we find out, through this experiment, to double how producing children in Germany? So you arrive at the men's lager.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe it for me, physically. What did the men's twin lager look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not a special twin lager. It was a hospital where they made all kinds of experiments. And with one of the experiments, were the experiment with twins. A human being was not worth a penny. He could take any person, any woman, any child, cut them in pieces, do any experiment. If he felt to take out your eye, he took out the eye. He was studying on your eye.

speaker: interviewer
text: He felt like to take a woman and just spread apart her like-- excuse me for the expression-- and do the worst thing with a woman. She was screaming, what are you doing? Made an experiment. Experiment! Cut from here a piece, cut from there a piece, it's nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we-- we were not even considered for animals, for nothing. Now about the Gypsies, before we start, because the Gypsies were the first, where they made experiments. He finished up the experiments, so we only saw, in the hospital, the pictures from the Gypsies, children, and the little bottles with the blood. They were finished up the experiment. It usually took a year's time. At the first, they were looking through x-rays, how the similar-- similarity is on twins, inside and outside. They took-- finished the experiment. Put them on the table, opened up the two twins, in pieces, like you open up a chicken today. And they studied on that. And that was the experiment. So they finished it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you will ask me, what kind of Gypsies were they? Because I know the Gypsies, from here, in America, and I know the Gypsies from my hometown, Munkatsh. They were a dirty, filthy, sick, cheap element.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those Gypsies, from Germany, who came there, and I was next to them. This was my camp. And be-- between the and between the Gypsies were a wire only. I did business, day and night, with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they brought them, doctors, lawyers, engineers, more intelligent, in a different type of element. Now probably you will ask me, how come? How come those are all the Gypsies? He found out, through the paper, everything. The German figured out, engineerly, the seven generation before was a Gypsy. Was he a Gypsy?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was like, as you, as I am. He was more intelligent than we are. But he found out, through the papers, the seven generation before was a Gypsy. First, he started with the Jews. Then he started with the Gypsies. Then he will take the colored people. All those people here, who are America, who thinks that they would survive. If, God forbid, Hitler would have won the war, there is not one colored person would have stayed alive, here, in America, and not in Africa, either. Because in his he's writing a book. All I need, people just with blond hair and blue eyes. And later, I'll tell you about the experiment, what kind of experiment he made, what is important to tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So those Gypsies, when they arrived with mink coats. They were not-- they-- they did not-- they didn't get the striped suits. They had a camp. The family was together. There was no-- how do you call it? [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It means, they didn't send them-- they didn't-- they didn't send them to-- to the gas chamber. They all stayed alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: No selection?

speaker: interviewee
text: No selection, right. You see, sometime the mind is blocked. There was no selection. They lived a nice family life. They had a canteen. They had, even for children, toys. The mothers and fathers could cook for themself. Once in a while, the men, they went to work. The women and the men didn't work. That's all what they was. They was like arrested in a-- in a camp. But they did not suffer like the rest of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then-- and what happened? The children, they used to get-- they wanted toys. And I used to get, from another camp, toys. And they gave me their food. And we-- and I made business, from morning to the evening. So actually, it was very convenient for you to be with them, together. One night, and the Russian already started to arrive, closer and closer to Auschwitz. They knew they have to finish up with the Gypsies.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they came with trucks. And they wanted to take them, one night, to the gas chamber. Of course, they knew everything, where they want to take them. They started to scream. They did not want to go on those closed trucks. And the Germans said, fine, don't have to go. Take it easy. You know everything was, by them, figured out engineerly. He said, no problem. You don't have to go on the truck. We just wanted to take you to make a bath. But if you don't want, you'll do it next time. Next day, they made it in a smart way. They took all the men, who is a really power. They took them that we have to straighten out some barracks, some physical work to do. So it means there was no more men. And the next day, evening, they came again with the trucks. This time, they had enough power to throw the women and the children on the truck. Next day, morning, ooh, the thousands of Gypsies are finished. No more Gypsy.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned-- you mentioned that you had traded with the Gypsies. You'd done some sort of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they had a canteen. And they could buy, in the canteen, food and toys and cigarette, mostly Majorca. Majorca was a tabac, but they made with paper. You made the cigarettes. And when I had a Majorca, I could buy, for myself, food. I could buy everything. And on one side, I had the Gypsy lager. On the other side was a lager they called it Kanada. Kanada was a lager where a few hundred women there selecting all the stuff, what the transport came, clothing separate, eyeglasses separate, shoes separate, jewelry separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I had a girl, who went me, too-- with me in the Hebrew gymnasium. And she used to throw me a package from there. What she threw me? She threw me ladies clothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I sold it, for the Gypsies, for food. Or I sold it for the kapos, who used to have dates with girls. They-- they couldn't bring candy, bonbons, so they brought ladies underwear. So I gave them the underwear. They gave me food. And I had a chance, the whole of the eight months to be there. First of all, I didn't took off my hair. I had a beautiful suit. They made-- made to order, one of the tailor. I didn't have to work, nothing. My job was the lageralteste. It means he was the main leader from the camp. He was Mengele's right-hand. He's Professor Epstein. When Professor Epstein arrived from Theresienstadt to Auschwitz, Mengele recognized him, that he was his professor, while he was studying, Mengele, in the university to be a doctor. And he took him out. And he made, from him, his right-hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall his first name, by any chance, Epstein?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Only I remember Epstein, an older man, could be approximately, already around between 60 or 70, white hair, very intelligent, a professor. And as the story goes on-- I wasn't there, but that's how they told me-- that when Mengele recognized him and took him out, he said, you're going to be my helper.

speaker: interviewer
text: You will help me out, here, because I was a doctor. So he asked, can I take my wife also? He says, no, your wife goes to a good place. It means-- So even so much, he didn't do for him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a band. He always dressed up, made to order. This striped suit, but this was mad from wool. He didn't shiver, in wintertime, from the thin suits. And he had the best food. He had a little office.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was responsible for the office. That like-- here is how do you call it? A messenger. I had to make sure that the room is clean. It was wintertime, it should be warm in the room. And when he came back with the paperwork, he gave me order, do this, do this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of the time, I had nothing to do. So that's how I had time to go and do business, send for my sister food, helping other people. And I was not very afraid for nothing. You see, if you went over to the wire, and the wires, between each camp, from one another, there was 5,000 electricity, in the wire.

speaker: interviewer
text: 5,000 volts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Volts, yeah, 5,000 volts. If it was a nice day, you could be at least two meters from the wire and didn't pull you there. But if it was a rainy day, from half a meter, pull you to the wire, and you was finished. In fact, one of the twins went to take a package, and, this time, we didn't know, if it's rainy day, it's dangerous. And the wire pulled him over, the whole back was burned.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he had a brother, a twin brother. And he used his mind, in the minute. He took a shovel. The shovel has a wood holder, right? And he pulled him away. And somehow, he survived it. And he stayed alive. So this was the experiment. Then we find out that, if it's a rainy day, don't go to the wire. So this Kanada, what I've told you is there. There, they had it was with mostly women that are there, not men only women. In the ladies' room, when-- there was a special kapo, a foreman, to take her on the ladies' room-- forgive me. Because if somebody didn't want to work too many hours, he went to the ladies' room just to waste time, not to do the work.

speaker: interviewee
text: But all of them, and as many as they opened up all this clothing, these shoes, in pockets, in buttons, in coats, in hats, every Jew brought, or a coin or a diamond. Instead of then-- they told them to report if for the Germans. All they did, everything went, they gave in some. Because they couldn't say nobody has it. But 90% of them, went to the toilet. In this toilet, what was diamonds there, you can buy a few Rockefeller Centers. And the later on, they was sending-- they was-- all of us, sending all kind of stuff, food or something. And one day, they caught one of the girl, who was throwing a package. She could be about 18, 19 years old. Came the German, a sadistic, young SS, maybe he was 20, 22 years old. And with a walking stick, she had to get, on behind, 25 lashes on her behind. Only the three, four lashes, she was screaming. She didn't screen any more. When they took out from the chair, not half-dead, but I would say 90% dead. Because they took her in, and the SS told it like this. Not for her, because she was not conscious. Tomorrow morning, if she's not attending to work, she goes to crematorium. But she was half-dead. The girls, they took her, with all the power, just not to go to crematorium. And he enjoyed it, just because she threw over a small piece of bread for somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were trading with the Gypsies--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what language did you speak with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: German. I knew the German language. I took some lessons in Hungary, German lessons. I knew German. And the Jewish language is very similar to the German. Of course, I was not fluent. In fact, it happened once a case, when I was by this Dr. Epstein's office. And one day, he told me that, bring me the waagen. I knew the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH] is a wagon. And waage is a scale. And I didn't concentrative. Bring me the waage, so I figured, bring me the wagon. And there were used to, if somebody died, there was a special room. And they picked him up, with this particular wagon, with two wheels. It was only with two wheels. And they brought them to the place, where, from there, they shipped them to the crematorium. He told me, bring me the waage, so right away, [NON-ENGLISH], and right away, like it's an order. And I bring him the wagon. And I go into the room. He's sitting by the bench. And he says, so bring it in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, I cannot bring it in, because the waage is bigger than the door. How can I bring it in? And he says, in German language, [GERMAN]? It means, are you crazy? You cannot bring a little scale in? But he didn't know that I'm being a wagon, that I-- I-- I'm not bringing a scale. So I started to laugh. That-- so the-- what I want to bring you out from this, I wasn't so good in German. I should know the difference between waage and a wagon. You understand? So that's what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this-- this Professor Epstein, when Mengele had no time to make a selection, he had to make the selection. You see, the four crematoriums had to burn 24 hours. There was a sonderkommando. If you ever heard about the sonderkommando, every six months, when we came a new transport, they took-- made a big, on the lautsprecher, on the microphone, doctors, engineers, intelligent people, dentists. And everybody, of course, was there to say, yes, I am. They took between 500 and 600 people. And they made the job when they came out from the gas chamber. They had to pull them out from the gas chamber-- they were already naked, anyway-- and put in this crematorium. A crematorium oven, you couldn't go in more than 25 person at once. As they say, the way I heard-- I saw, later, the crematorium-- it took about 20 minutes to burn the body. Of course, there was leftover bones, whatever. But-- so their work was, day and night, to feed the crematorium with bodies. And two doctors, who are from my hometown, Dr. Havash and Dr. Peter, when they was working, daytime, in the crematorium-- I mean, the nighttime-- daytime they came and they was cutting the grass. So I was next to them and had a right to talk to one of the doctors. And they told me all the stories what was going on. Now they also told me what happened. In the gas chamber was officially room for 1,500 people to be gassed. But because they were afraid that the Russian were coming closer, they pushed 1,800 and sometimes 2,000 people in the same gas chamber. And when they opened up the door, another 200 or 300 of us was still-- 100 people were still alive. And they went with the next shipment to the gas chamber. What he told me, at this time, I couldn't understand how can he-- how a Jewish doctor could do such a work, to recognize, here, his sister. When-- when he took out there to recognize one of the family. But they did it. Now they had to be changed every six months, because the plan was figured out by the Germans. If they, let's say-- they hoped they would win the war. But in case they lose the war, so one guy who's doing this work will be able to tell, for all the four years, what happened in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if they see only the last 500-- and they hope, the last 500, also to kill before the Russian or whoever, the American, will arrive-- not to tell the story of what was going on. You see, each person had a number. They had bookkeeping.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- at first it was 200,000. Then they started A 200,000. And they started, and then B 200,000. So all those people, who were, right away, destroyed, there's no record. There's no such a thing. I was-- never happened. But those who are numbers, there's a record. He was doing this here. Everything to show for the world. They invited, once, the Red Cross to Auschwitz. And you should see, in the same day, what kind of meal they gave everybody. The linens were white. The food was like in a sanatorium. And we showed that, you knows, everything is fine. [? We, ?] [? too, ?] [? with ?] [? we ?] Germans, we wouldn't hurt a fly!

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you there when the Red Cross came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. And because I was in this barrack. Now, between all the people who arrived to Auschwitz, as they say, from 22 nations people arrived, only the Jew was selected. Any other person who died, died a natural death. If you died from a sickness, if you died from weakness, if you died from a cold, if you had to come to the hospital, he arrived to a hospital. You had to give them help in a hospital, whatever they had there. But selected were only the Jew. Later, by the end, was the Gypsies. So as we are here today, in America, and all over the world, we Jews, we should know one thing. If you are a Zionist, if you have feeling to Israel or not, you should know one thing. Only two people were destroyed in life in Auschwitz. And those were the Jews and the Gypsies, because they didn't have a country. So when we think about Israel, and we think to give something back for the Arabs or take all these lies from Arafat, and he only wants the south part or the east part from Jerusalem, this is all lies. He wants to take over Israel. All the Arabs, they want to take over Israel. Because, if you would come an Indian, right now, who was born and his great-great-father was born in America, and who come to Washington, we say, now, we want to have the east part of Washington should be our capital.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would say our government? Get the hell out of here, right? Would you talk about it? Would you discuss it? The Americans are only 500 years in this country since Columbus. Why don't they give them a part from Washington for a capital town? Because it's nonsense. There's no such a thing. You ever heard, in a capital town, to do for another nation a capital town, part of the capital town? This is all excuses for the-- to the Arabs.

speaker: interviewee
text: They just-- you see the world felt guilty after the war to give us Israeli state. Because they knew they did the dirty thing. Not only the Germans, included Americans, included the whole world. I don't like communism. But one thing I want to tell you, I have respect for the-- the Russians. They were the only, in Europe, who opened up their-- their borders. And they said, come there and be there. All the Polish people, who wanted to go to Russia, they could go. Not England, didn't open up the door. Not America didn't open up the door. The ship was here. They send it back. They knew, the Americans, where the ship, it goes. They go all back to Germany, into the concentration camp. But the Russian, they gave an order, even they are not so intelligent then the West is. Every person, when there comes a newcomer to Russia, and if you one potato, divide it with this refugee. I don't say the Russians were good to the Jews. I don't say they liked the Jews. But they were the only one who opened up the borders. And I respect them for this. I don't like them, but I respect them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the sleeping arrangements like, where you were staying, in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I told you before, I, myself, like a twin. And even, I had better than the rest of the twins, because I have an office. I have my own room. I live like in a hotel. I ate the best and the nicest. I used to cook for myself. I used to have the hair. I used to have the best that I had. This was eight months, until I was in Auschwitz. Then I went away, in January, and arrived to Mauthausen. This is another story I will have to start to tell you. So I, myself, personally I can't complain. And if I wouldn't have this saved energy, what I saved up in this eight months there, I wouldn't survive Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because when I came to Mauthausen, I was like I came out from a-- from a sanatorium, you understand? And even those [? other ?] [? twin, ?] not all the twins had it. I was not afraid for nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would never have believed, in my life, that I'd ever be free again. So even if they caught me, even if they kill me, you see, life was nothing. And I had the pleasure to help the rest of the twins. We had twins from five year old, three years old. They were hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I helped them. I have, here, a boy who's, right now, in Switzerland, one of the biggest manufacturer in the watches. He was, this time, 10 years old. And he was very hungry. He was a chubby child.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't want to hurt his feelings. And I had always enough food, because I made business. So I said, listen, evening we came together, all the children, and children that were crying. They was thinking about the mother. They was thinking about the father. You know, growing up, think different, and a child is thinking different. I said, listen, I'll give you a nice slice of bread, big, beautiful margarine. And even if I give you any sweet stuff on it, but I want you should dance for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the children around started to laugh. And he was happy. He got another slice of bread. Not all of the twins had everything. We have more than the general, average public, but not all of them. So you had your own separate room, where you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Separate room. I was the messenger for Dr. Epstein. And I lived like a king there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far from the actual barracks, where the rest of the twins were, were you staying?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in the same camp. But they were in a barrack that was only twins. Approximately about 50 or 60, because, all together, we were, boys and girls, around 100. So I would say about 50, 60 boys were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll end tape number three here. We'll pick this up with tape number four.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
